items.question: What is histogram-based split finding in GBDT libraries?
items.answer: Numeric features are binned into a fixed number of buckets; gradient statistics are aggregated per bin and splits are evaluated only at bin boundaries. This reduces split-search cost versus exact greedy methods on every unique value.

items.question: How does leaf-wise growth differ from level-wise growth?
items.answer: Leaf-wise (LightGBM default) splits the leaf with the largest loss reduction next, producing asymmetric trees controlled by num_leaves. Level-wise (XGBoost style) splits all leaves at the same depth each round, controlled by max_depth.

items.question: How do XGBoost and LightGBM handle missing values?
items.answer: Both learn a default branch direction at each split for missing entries during training. At prediction time, NaN follows that learned direction — no imputation required, though explicit preprocessing may still help when missingness is informative.

items.question: Why use early stopping with gradient boosting?
items.answer: Validation loss often improves for early rounds then worsens as trees overfit training residuals. Early stopping halts at best_iteration, preventing late useless trees when using a fixed learning rate.

items.question: Name three important XGBoost hyperparameters and what they control.
items.answer: learning_rate (shrinkage per tree), max_depth (level-wise depth cap), reg_lambda/reg_alpha (L2/L1 regularization on leaf weights). Also common: subsample, colsample_bytree, min_child_weight.

items.question: When might LightGBM overfit more easily than XGBoost with default growth policies?
items.answer: Leaf-wise growth with high num_leaves and low min_data_in_leaf on small data can create deep paths fitting noise. Level-wise max_depth caps are more conservative by default — always validate and regularize.
